title: could not start api proxy since expected pipe is not available: podman-westie
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

On Windows 11 under WSL-2 when starting machine I'm getting the error

      could not start api proxy since expected pipe is not available: podman-westie

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. In powershell run: podman machine start westie.

**Describe the results you received:**

Starting machine "westie"
API forwarding for Docker API clients is not available due to the following startup failures.
        could not start api proxy since expected pipe is not available: podman-westie

Podman clients are still able to connect.
Machine "westie" started successfully

**Describe the results you expected:**

machine would start without proxy error



**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

comments.body:
After running 

 podman machine stop westie

podman machine start westie

I got the expected result, where can I find info on what the error means any suggestions on how to debug why this issue happens I have seen this issue a few times now

podman machine start westie
Starting machine "westie"
API forwarding listening on: npipe:////./pipe/docker_engine

Docker API clients default to this address. You do not need to set DOCKER_HOST.
Machine "westie" started successfully

Since the pipe file it’s complaining about is the machine named file this would mean that a previous run was still holding the pipe file. There is two possibilities where that might happen. One is a race, where the exiting process still held the file briefly. The other possibility is that you had a stale win-sshproxy process that was somehow still running (it’s normally shut down as part of stop)

Since it cleared up after a restart it sounds more like a race. Are you automating start and stop with scripting or running in two terminal Windows? If you were seeing this just from serial start and stop in the same terminal session, there’s a possibility there are conditions Windows is delayed in releasing the pipe after process exit. If that is indeed the case we could look at retrying to resolve the race (at least for the machine named pipe)

Many thanks for the info, much appreciated. I was just manually starting and stopping the machine, I will see if I can figure out more about the conditions under which this is happening and update the bug report

I'm getting the same behaviour, but seem to get the connection back with the following;

```powershell
wsl --shutdown
get-service | where DisplayName -like '*docker*'
podman machine start # Must be from an Administrator terminal. Users still get "CreateFile \\.\pipe\docker_engine: All pipe instances are busy"
```
* note `wsl -d podman-machine-default --terminate` untested if you wanted to be specific about the distro you shutdown.

I didn't need to restart hyper-v but that was my next try. I opened/closed docker-desktop just to see if it would clear something so stoppping the docker service might be a red herring. I wasn't using docker at all last week when I fixed this, just using administrator for the start command was enough, but not this time.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I looked into this and see the underlying problem. Will follow up with a PR
I seem to randomly run into this from time to time. Recreating the podman machine is the only thing I've found to resolve this. Is there another way to find what's using the pipe and kill it?
